citation: creatingattrpgresources2023: Resources for Creating a TTRPG
summary:
# Summary

The provided text, titled "Resources for Creating a TTRPG," functions as a preparatory guide and resource compilation for individuals seeking to design Tabletop Role-Playing Games (TTRPGs). While the metadata suggests a 2023 origin and an association with library services (via references to GameRT/ALA), the document itself is not an empirical study but rather a curated list of tools, terminology, and reflective notes. Its primary purpose is to equip creators—specifically those with limited prior experience in game design—with the necessary foundational knowledge and external resources to begin the creation process.

The document begins by defining key terms relevant to the field, including "RPG," "TTRPG," "micro rpg," "solo rpg," "Game Jam," "hack," "Open Game System (OGS)," and "Creative Commons license." This terminology section establishes the conceptual framework for the resources that follow. The core of the document is a structured list of external resources categorized by type. These include online articles and threads on game design (e.g., from Dicebreaker and Avery Alder), platforms for publishing games such as Itch.io, and video content like the Miscast episode "Creating My Own Tabletop Role-Playing Game." The inclusion of image resources from the Biodiversity Heritage Library and the Library of Congress highlights the availability of public domain visual assets for game development.

A significant portion of the resource list is dedicated to technical and safety tools. Technical aids mentioned include Google Jamboard, suggesting a digital or hybrid approach to design collaboration. More notably, the document emphasizes player safety and consent mechanisms, listing specific tools such as "Script Change" by Beau Jágr Sheldon, "The Door is Open" by Eirik Fatland, "Lines & Veils" by Ron Edwards, and the comprehensive "TTRPG Safety Toolkit." This focus on safety underscores an ethical consideration in game design, particularly relevant for library settings where diverse patrons may engage with potentially sensitive content.

The document also provides a list of TTRPGs for inspiration, including *Microscope* by Ben Robbins, *A Mending* by Shing Yin Khor, and *Space Rock Opera* by Andrew J. Young. Additionally, it lists various industry awards (Ennies, Indie, Origins, Diana Jones, etc.) as sources of inspiration, indicating a desire to engage with high-quality or recognized works in the field.

In the "Points To Discuss" section, the authors reflect on their own positionality. They explicitly state that they "don’t know what we are doing" and have not conducted in-depth research into game creation. However, they frame this lack of expertise as a potential advantage, noting the "value of creating without rules or preconceived notions." They express a strong willingness to experiment ("a lot of will to create") and view the process as a means to empathize with patrons ("we’ll be in the shoes of many of our patrons"). This suggests that the document is part of a broader initiative, likely within a library context, to use game creation as a method for professional development and patron engagement.

The implications drawn by the authors are forward-looking. They anticipate that the challenges they encounter during the creation process will inform future research directions ("Going forward will know what to research as we consider where we struggled"). This positions the document not just as a static resource list, but as a starting point for experiential learning and subsequent inquiry into games-based library services. The text concludes with contact information for GameRT (Gaming Round Table of the American Library Association), reinforcing its connection to professional library practice. Overall, the document serves as a bridge between theoretical interest in TTRPGs and practical application, providing a scaffold for novice creators while highlighting the importance of safety, open resources, and empathetic design.
sourceText:
Resources for Creating a TTRPG
Terms to Explore Role playing game, RPG, TTRPG, micro rpg, solo rpg, Game Jam, hack, OGS, Open Game System, Creative Commons license, ttrpg zine, game design diary
Open Game Systems ● https://wiki.rpg.net/index.php/Open_Game_Systems
Online Resources ● Dicebreaker, article: How to make a roleplaying game ● Avery Alder, twitter thread: Tools for tabletop roleplaying design ● Website Itch.io ● #RPGSEA
Videos ● Miscast: Creating My Own Tabletop Role-Playing Game
Image Resources ● Biodiversity Heritage Library Flickr ● LOC: Free to Use and Reuse
TTRPGs Referenced
● Microscope by Ben Robbins, ● A Mending by Shing Yin Khor, ● Space Rock Opera by Andrew J. Young ● Games by Sealed Library
Technical Tools ● Google Jamboard
Safety Tools ● Script Change by Beau Jágr Sheldon ● The Door is Open by Eirik Fatland ○ Blog by Lizzie Stark ○ Blog 2 by Eirik Fatland ● TTRPG Safety Toolkit collected by Kienna Shaw and Lauren Bryant-Monk ○ TTRPG Safety Toolkit Guide ○ Google Drive

● Building the Pallette by Ben Robbins ● Lines & Veils by Ron Edwards
RPG Awards for Inspiration ● Ennies ● Indie ● Origins Award ● Tabletop Gaming Award ● Tabletop Awards Dicebreaker ● Diana Jones Award Gen Con ● TTRPGS nominated for the Nebula
Learn More About GameRT Blog: bit.ly/GameRTBlog Facebook: bit.ly/GameRTLeague & bit.ly/GameRTFacebook Resources: bit.ly/GameRTPublicResources Website: bit.ly/GameRTWebsite Questions? Email gamert@ala.org ___ Points To Discuss
● Don’t know what we are doing, and have not researched in depth ● Have played and read and encountered a bunch of games ● This will be great as we’ll be in the shoes of many of our patrons
○ Not much experience but a lot of will to create ○ The value of creating without rules or preconceived notions ○ Allowing experimentation in our lives ○ Playing with playing ● Going forward will know what to research as we consider where we struggled or what we want to know more about
